eliminated.name: Hassan Nasrallah
eliminated.title: Secretary-General of Hezbollah
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force bunker-buster strike
eliminated.significance: Led Hezbollah for 32 years since 1992. Transformed Hezbollah into the most powerful non-state military force in the Middle East. Under his leadership, Hezbollah amassed 150,000+ rockets and missiles. His elimination marked the most significant blow to Iran's proxy network in decades.

eliminated.name: Ismail Haniyeh
eliminated.title: Chairman of the Hamas Political Bureau
eliminated.method: Targeted assassination — explosive device in Tehran guesthouse
eliminated.significance: Head of Hamas' political bureau since 2017. Former Prime Minister of the Palestinian Authority (2006-2007). Served as Hamas' chief diplomat and fundraiser, managing relationships with Iran, Qatar, and Turkey. Killed while attending the inauguration of Iran's new president.

eliminated.name: Fuad Shukr
eliminated.title: Senior Military Commander, Hezbollah
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli precision strike
eliminated.significance: Hezbollah's most senior military commander after Nasrallah. Responsible for Hezbollah's strategic weapons program. US had a $5 million Rewards for Justice bounty on him. Linked to the 1983 Beirut barracks bombing that killed 241 US Marines.

eliminated.name: Saleh al-Arouri
eliminated.title: Deputy Chairman of Hamas Political Bureau; Founder of West Bank military infrastructure
eliminated.method: Airstrike — drone strike attributed to Israel
eliminated.significance: Founded Hamas' military operations in the West Bank. Key liaison between Hamas and Hezbollah in Lebanon. Believed to be involved in planning the October 7 attack. His killing in Beirut demonstrated Israel's reach into Hezbollah's stronghold.

eliminated.name: Ayman al-Zawahiri
eliminated.title: Emir (Leader) of Al-Qaeda
eliminated.method: CIA drone strike — R9X Hellfire missile (kinetic, no warhead)
eliminated.operationName: CIA operation — authorized by President Biden
eliminated.significance: Succeeded Osama bin Laden as Al-Qaeda leader in 2011. Egyptian surgeon who co-founded Egyptian Islamic Jihad. Key planner of 9/11 attacks. His presence in Taliban-controlled Kabul proved the Taliban had not severed ties with Al-Qaeda as promised in the 2020 Doha Agreement.

eliminated.name: Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi
eliminated.title: Caliph / Leader of the Islamic State
eliminated.method: US Special Operations raid — Delta Force (detonated suicide vest during raid)
eliminated.significance: Declared the ISIS 'caliphate' from the Great Mosque of al-Nuri in Mosul, June 2014. Under his leadership, ISIS controlled territory the size of Britain, generated $1B+ annually, and inspired terrorist attacks worldwide. The most wanted terrorist in the world at the time of his death.

eliminated.name: Qasem Soleimani
eliminated.title: Commander of the IRGC Quds Force
eliminated.method: US drone strike — MQ-9 Reaper firing Hellfire missiles
eliminated.significance: Iran's most powerful military figure. Architect of Iran's proxy network across the Middle East. Directed operations by Hezbollah, Shia militias in Iraq, Houthis, and Assad regime support. His assassination by the US was the most significant targeted killing since Osama bin Laden. Nearly triggered direct US-Iran war.

eliminated.name: Osama bin Laden
eliminated.title: Founder and Emir of Al-Qaeda
eliminated.method: US Special Operations raid — SEAL Team Six (DEVGRU)
eliminated.significance: Mastermind of the September 11, 2001 attacks that killed 2,977 people. Founded Al-Qaeda in 1988. The most wanted person in the world for nearly a decade. His killing by Navy SEALs in a compound near Pakistan's military academy was one of the most significant special operations in history.

eliminated.name: Abu Ibrahim al-Hashimi al-Qurayshi
eliminated.title: Leader of the Islamic State (2nd Caliph)
eliminated.method: US Special Operations raid — detonated suicide vest
eliminated.operationName: US counterterrorism operation — authorized by President Biden
eliminated.significance: Succeeded al-Baghdadi as ISIS leader. Maintained the organization's global insurgency network despite territorial losses. Killed in a US raid similar to the al-Baghdadi operation.

eliminated.name: Ibrahim Aqil
eliminated.title: Commander of Hezbollah's Radwan Force (elite special operations unit)
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force precision strike on Beirut
eliminated.operationName: Israeli targeted strike during Hezbollah military meeting
eliminated.significance: Commander of Hezbollah's elite Radwan Force. US had a $7 million bounty for his role in the 1983 US Embassy bombing and Marine barracks bombing in Beirut. Killed alongside multiple other Radwan Force commanders in what Israel described as a strike on a meeting of senior military leadership.

eliminated.name: Yevgeny Prigozhin
eliminated.title: Founder and Leader of Wagner Group
eliminated.method: Aircraft crash — Embraer Legacy business jet shot down (alleged assassination)
eliminated.location: Tver Oblast, Russia (in-flight between Moscow and St. Petersburg)
eliminated.operationName: Widely attributed to Russian state / GRU
eliminated.significance: Founded the Wagner Group private military company. Led the failed June 2023 mutiny against Russian military leadership. Exactly two months after the mutiny, his plane crashed killing all aboard. His death consolidated Russian state control over Wagner operations, which were rebranded as 'Africa Corps' under GRU command.

eliminated.name: Mohammed Deif
eliminated.title: Commander of the Izz ad-Din al-Qassam Brigades (Hamas military wing)
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force strike using multiple munitions
eliminated.significance: Supreme commander of Hamas' military wing for over 20 years. Mastermind of the October 7, 2023 attack. Survived at least 7 previous Israeli assassination attempts, losing an eye, an arm, and the ability to walk. The most wanted person in Israel for decades.

eliminated.name: Marwan Issa
eliminated.title: Deputy Commander of Izz ad-Din al-Qassam Brigades
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force strike on underground compound
eliminated.significance: Known as the 'Shadow Man' — Hamas' third most senior military leader. Deputy to Mohammed Deif. Key planner of the October 7 attack. Oversaw Hamas' tunnel network and weapons development programs.

eliminated.name: Abu Hussein al-Hamidawi
eliminated.title: Leader of the Islamic State (4th)
eliminated.method: Turkish intelligence operation (MIT)
eliminated.significance: Succeeded Abu al-Hassan al-Hashimi al-Qurayshi as ISIS leader. Led ISIS for approximately 7 months before being killed, demonstrating the group's difficulty in maintaining stable leadership under constant pressure from US and allied forces.

eliminated.name: Hashem Safieddine
eliminated.title: Head of Hezbollah's Executive Council; Expected successor to Nasrallah
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force strike on underground bunker in Beirut
eliminated.significance: Nasrallah's cousin and the most likely successor to lead Hezbollah. Headed Hezbollah's Executive Council, managing the organization's financial and administrative operations. His killing days after Nasrallah's assassination further decapitated Hezbollah's leadership structure.

eliminated.name: Abubakar Shekau
eliminated.title: Leader of Boko Haram
eliminated.method: Suicide — detonated explosive vest during ISWAP assault on his position
eliminated.operationName: ISWAP assault on Boko Haram stronghold
eliminated.significance: Notorious leader of Boko Haram since 2009. Orchestrated the 2014 kidnapping of 276 Chibok schoolgirls. Under his leadership, Boko Haram killed over 20,000 people. Killed himself with a suicide vest rather than surrender to rival ISWAP fighters, leading to Boko Haram's fragmentation.

eliminated.name: Yahya Sinwar
eliminated.title: Chairman of the Hamas Political Bureau; Leader of Hamas in Gaza
eliminated.method: IDF ground engagement — killed by Israeli soldiers during building clearance in Rafah
eliminated.significance: Mastermind and operational commander of the October 7, 2023 attack that killed ~1,200 Israelis. Rose through Hamas' ranks during 22 years in Israeli prison. Known as 'The Butcher of Khan Younis.' Israel's #1 most wanted individual. Found alone in a partially destroyed building by an IDF patrol — not in the tunnel network as expected. His death marked the elimination of the most senior architect of the October 7 massacre.

eliminated.name: Rafa'a Salameh
eliminated.title: Commander of Hamas' Khan Younis Brigade (Qassam Brigades)
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force strike using multiple munitions
eliminated.operationName: IDF targeted strike (same strike that killed Mohammed Deif)
eliminated.significance: Commander of Hamas' Khan Younis Brigade, one of the five main territorial brigades of the Qassam Brigades. Killed in the same massive Israeli airstrike that targeted Mohammed Deif. Responsible for Hamas military operations across the Khan Younis governorate.

eliminated.name: Ahmed Ghandour
eliminated.title: Commander of Hamas' Northern Gaza Division (Qassam Brigades)
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force precision strike
eliminated.significance: Commander of Hamas' Northern Gaza Division, overseeing all Qassam Brigades operations in northern Gaza including the Beit Hanoun, Beit Lahia, and Jabalia battalions. Played a key role in the October 7 attack planning. One of the most senior Hamas military commanders eliminated in the war.

eliminated.name: Ayman Nofal
eliminated.title: Commander of Hamas' Central Gaza Brigade (Qassam Brigades)
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force precision strike
eliminated.significance: Commander of Hamas' Central Camps Brigade, one of the first senior Hamas military commanders killed in the war. Led the Qassam Brigades' operations in the Bureij, Nuseirat, and Maghazi refugee camps. His early elimination disrupted Hamas' central command structure.

eliminated.name: Ibrahim Biari
eliminated.title: Commander of Hamas' Shuja'iyya Battalion (Qassam Brigades)
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force strike on Jabalia refugee camp area
eliminated.significance: Commanded the Qassam Brigades' Shuja'iyya Battalion, one of the key units in Gaza City. Involved in planning and execution of the October 7 breach of the Gaza border fence. His battalion was responsible for multiple infiltration routes used on October 7.

eliminated.name: Wissam Farhat
eliminated.title: Commander of Hamas' Central Gaza Division (succeeded Ayman Nofal)
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force precision strike
eliminated.significance: Took command of Hamas' Central Gaza Division after the elimination of Ayman Nofal. His killing within months of assuming command demonstrated Israel's ability to identify and target Hamas' replacement commanders.

eliminated.name: Rawhi Mushtaha
eliminated.title: Senior Hamas Leader; Head of Hamas' Government Administration in Gaza
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force strike on underground compound
eliminated.significance: One of Yahya Sinwar's closest associates and a founding member of Hamas. Managed Hamas' governmental operations in Gaza and was a member of Hamas' senior leadership circle. His relationship with Sinwar dated back to their time together in Israeli prison.

eliminated.name: Sameh al-Siraj
eliminated.title: Head of Hamas' General Security Mechanism
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force strike on underground compound
eliminated.operationName: IDF targeted strike (killed alongside Rawhi Mushtaha)
eliminated.significance: Led Hamas' internal security apparatus, responsible for counterintelligence, preventing collaboration with Israel, and maintaining organizational discipline. Key figure in Hamas' security infrastructure.

eliminated.name: Zachariah Najib
eliminated.title: Head of Hamas' Internal Security Service
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force strike on underground compound
eliminated.operationName: IDF targeted strike (killed alongside Mushtaha and al-Siraj)
eliminated.significance: Oversaw Hamas' internal security operations. His elimination alongside Mushtaha and al-Siraj in the same strike represented a major blow to Hamas' security and administrative leadership — three senior leaders killed in a single operation.

eliminated.name: Fateh Sherif Abu el-Amin
eliminated.title: Head of Hamas' operations in Lebanon
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force strike
eliminated.operationName: Israeli targeted strike during Lebanon campaign
eliminated.significance: Led Hamas' political and operational presence in Lebanon. Coordinated between Hamas and Hezbollah. His killing in Lebanon demonstrated the expansion of Israel's targeting to Hamas leadership outside Gaza.

eliminated.name: Ghazi Abu Tammam
eliminated.title: Head of Hamas' Intelligence and Counterintelligence Directorate
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force precision strike
eliminated.significance: Oversaw Hamas' intelligence apparatus, responsible for intelligence collection, surveillance, and counterintelligence efforts against Israeli penetration of the organization.

eliminated.name: Izz al-Din Haddad
eliminated.title: Head of Hamas' Weapons Development and Manufacturing
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force precision strike on weapons facility
eliminated.significance: Oversaw Hamas' domestic weapons manufacturing program, including rocket production, drone development, and IED fabrication. Developed the capability to produce thousands of rockets locally and naval commando equipment used in the October 7 attack.

eliminated.name: Jawad Abu Shamala
eliminated.title: Head of Hamas' Finance and Economic Portfolio
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force strike
eliminated.significance: Managed Hamas' financial operations, overseeing the movement of funds from Iran and other state sponsors into Gaza. Controlled Hamas' investment portfolio and resource allocation between military and civilian operations.

eliminated.name: Osama Mazini
eliminated.title: Member of Hamas Political Bureau; Hostage Negotiations Lead
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force precision strike
eliminated.significance: Senior member of Hamas' political bureau and a key figure in hostage negotiations following the October 7 attack. Played a central role in Hamas' strategy regarding the Israeli hostages.

eliminated.name: Hassan Qahoush
eliminated.title: Head of Hamas Intelligence — Northern Gaza
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force precision strike during ground operation
eliminated.significance: Led Hamas' intelligence operations in northern Gaza. Key intelligence figure in the October 7 attack planning. Responsible for surveillance of Israeli military positions and planning infiltration operations.

eliminated.name: Bilal al-Kedra
eliminated.title: Commander of Hamas Operations — Khan Younis sector
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force precision strike
eliminated.operationName: IDF targeted strike during Khan Younis operation
eliminated.significance: Senior Hamas military commander in the Khan Younis area. Oversaw tactical operations, tunnel warfare, and anti-tank ambush teams targeting IDF forces during the ground incursion.

eliminated.name: Murad Abu Murad
eliminated.title: Deputy Commander of Hamas' Khan Younis Brigade
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force strike on command facility
eliminated.significance: Second-in-command of the Khan Younis Brigade under Rafa'a Salameh. Managed tactical operations and coordinated ambush teams against IDF ground forces.

eliminated.name: Tayseer Mubasher
eliminated.title: Battalion Commander — Khan Younis (Qassam Brigades)
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force strike
eliminated.significance: Commanded a battalion-level unit within the Khan Younis Brigade. Oversaw rocket launching operations and underground tunnel warfare against IDF forces.

eliminated.name: Adel Mismah
eliminated.title: Deputy Commander of Hamas' Northern Gaza Brigade
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force strike during ground operation
eliminated.significance: Deputy to Ahmed Ghandour in the Northern Gaza Division. Managed tactical operations in the Jabalia and Beit Hanoun areas during the intense fighting in northern Gaza.

eliminated.name: Zakaria Abu Ma'amar
eliminated.title: Member of Hamas Political Bureau — Southern Gaza
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force precision strike on residence
eliminated.significance: Member of Hamas' political bureau responsible for political operations in southern Gaza. His killing targeted Hamas' political leadership alongside its military commanders.

eliminated.name: Ahmad al-Mansi
eliminated.title: Commander of Hamas' Shuja'iyya Operations
eliminated.method: IDF ground operation and airstrike support
eliminated.significance: Oversaw Qassam Brigades operations in the Shuja'iyya neighborhood of Gaza City, one of the most fiercely contested urban battlegrounds with extensive tunnel infrastructure.

eliminated.name: Nidal Khamis
eliminated.title: Commander of Hamas' Training Programs (Qassam Brigades)
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force precision strike
eliminated.significance: Oversaw the training and preparation of Qassam Brigades fighters. Responsible for the Nukhba commando training program whose graduates carried out the October 7 infiltration.

eliminated.name: Hamza Abu el-Ouf
eliminated.title: Head of Hamas' Logistics and Equipment Division
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force strike on logistics compound
eliminated.significance: Managed Hamas' logistics network including weapons storage, supply routes through the tunnel system, and equipment distribution across Gaza's underground infrastructure.

eliminated.name: Ahmad Mubarak
eliminated.title: Commander of Hamas' Rocket Unit — Northern Gaza
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force strike on rocket launching position
eliminated.significance: Led Hamas' rocket launching operations in northern Gaza. Responsible for directing Qassam and modified rocket fire toward Israeli cities including Sderot and Ashkelon.

eliminated.name: Amin al-Hafi
eliminated.title: Commander of Hamas Operations — Shuja'iyya Sector
eliminated.method: IDF ground operation — killed during urban combat
eliminated.significance: Senior commander responsible for Hamas' defensive operations in Shuja'iyya. Coordinated anti-armor ambushes and booby-trapped building operations against advancing IDF forces.

eliminated.name: Mohammed Shabaneh
eliminated.title: Head of Hamas' West Bank Military Operations
eliminated.method: IDF special operations raid
eliminated.significance: Oversaw Hamas' military infrastructure and attack planning in the West Bank. Coordinated weapons smuggling, recruitment, and attack cells following the model created by Saleh al-Arouri.

eliminated.name: Hussam Badran
eliminated.title: Hamas Media Office Chief; Senior Political Figure
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force precision strike
eliminated.significance: Long-time head of Hamas' media and communications operations. Managed the organization's public messaging and propaganda. Previously convicted of directing suicide bombings during the Second Intifada.

eliminated.name: Issam al-Da'alis
eliminated.title: Head of Hamas' Internal Security — Southern Gaza
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force strike on security compound
eliminated.operationName: IDF targeted strike during Rafah operation
eliminated.significance: Commanded Hamas' internal security apparatus in southern Gaza. Responsible for counterintelligence operations and maintaining discipline within Hamas' ranks during wartime.

eliminated.name: Muhammad Abu Shamala
eliminated.title: Commander of Hamas' Rafah Brigade (Qassam Brigades)
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force strike during Rafah operation
eliminated.operationName: IDF targeted strike during Rafah ground operation
eliminated.significance: Commanded the Qassam Brigades' Rafah Brigade. Oversaw the tunnel networks connecting Gaza to the Egyptian border, including smuggling tunnels used for weapons importation.

eliminated.name: Samer Abu Daqqa
eliminated.title: Deputy Commander of Hamas' Qassam Brigades — Khan Younis
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force precision strike
eliminated.significance: Senior deputy commander within the Khan Younis Brigade command structure. Oversaw tactical operations and anti-tank warfare teams deployed against IDF armored units.

eliminated.name: Ahmed Shahwan
eliminated.title: Head of Hamas Military Police
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force strike
eliminated.significance: Commanded Hamas' military police force. Played a role in the management and guarding of hostages taken during the October 7 attack.

eliminated.name: Nasser Madi
eliminated.title: Commander of Nukhba Commando Unit — Northern Sector
eliminated.method: IDF ground operation — killed during close-quarters combat
eliminated.operationName: IDF ground operation in northern Gaza
eliminated.significance: Led a company-level element of Hamas' elite Nukhba commando force — the spearhead of the October 7 attack. Madi's unit was responsible for attacks on multiple kibbutzim near the border.

eliminated.name: Raed Saad
eliminated.title: Commander of Hamas' Tunnel Operations — Gaza City
eliminated.method: IDF ground operation — killed during tunnel demolition operation
eliminated.significance: Oversaw the construction and operation of Hamas' tunnel network beneath Gaza City — the 'Gaza Metro' — extending for hundreds of kilometers as Hamas' primary defensive infrastructure.

eliminated.name: Abed Fattah al-Sharif
eliminated.title: Commander of Hamas Anti-Tank Operations — Gaza Division
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force strike on concealed firing position
eliminated.significance: Led Hamas' anti-tank guided missile (ATGM) operations. Under his command, Hamas deployed Kornet and Al-Yassin 105 anti-tank weapons that disabled multiple IDF Merkava tanks and Namer APCs during the ground campaign.

eliminated.name: Dia al-Din al-Qassam
eliminated.title: Commander of Hamas Naval Commando Unit
eliminated.method: Israeli Navy operation — killed during coastal interdiction
eliminated.significance: Led Hamas' naval commando unit used during the October 7 attack to infiltrate Israeli territory via sea. The naval commando route was one of three axes of the October 7 attack.

eliminated.name: Khaled Mansour
eliminated.title: Commander of Hamas' Southern Brigade (Qassam Brigades)
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force strike during Rafah operation
eliminated.significance: Senior commander of Qassam Brigades operations in the southern Gaza sector. Oversaw cross-border tunnel operations and weapons smuggling routes connecting Gaza to Sinai.

eliminated.name: Suhail al-Hindi
eliminated.title: Head of Hamas' Shura Council; Political Guidance
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force precision strike
eliminated.significance: Led Hamas' Shura (consultative) Council, the organization's senior religious and strategic advisory body. A founding-era figure of Hamas who provided religious rulings and strategic guidance.

eliminated.name: Mohammed Sinwar
eliminated.title: Commander of Hamas' Qassam Brigades in Khan Younis; Brother of Yahya Sinwar
eliminated.method: IDF ground operation — killed during tunnel clearance operation
eliminated.significance: Brother of Hamas leader Yahya Sinwar and a senior military commander. Considered a key link in maintaining Hamas' command structure after the elimination of more senior leaders. His death further dismantled the Sinwar family's grip on Hamas' military operations.

eliminated.name: Abbas al-Musawi
eliminated.title: Secretary-General of Hezbollah (predecessor to Nasrallah)
eliminated.method: Israeli helicopter gunship strike — Apache AH-64 firing Hellfire missiles
eliminated.operationName: IDF targeted strike on Hezbollah leadership convoy
eliminated.significance: Secretary-General of Hezbollah from 1991-1992. His assassination by Israel led to the appointment of Hassan Nasrallah, who would go on to lead Hezbollah for 32 years. The killing was a significant early example of Israel's targeted assassination policy against Hezbollah leadership.

eliminated.name: Imad Mughniyeh
eliminated.title: Head of Hezbollah's Islamic Jihad Organization (military/intelligence chief)
eliminated.method: Car bomb — joint CIA/Mossad operation, explosive embedded in headrest
eliminated.significance: The most wanted terrorist in the world before bin Laden. Mastermind of the 1983 Beirut Marine barracks bombing (241 killed), 1983 US Embassy bombing, TWA Flight 847 hijacking, Buenos Aires AMIA bombing (85 killed), and Khobar Towers bombing. Operated Hezbollah's global terror network for 25 years. His elimination was one of the most significant joint US-Israeli intelligence operations ever conducted.

eliminated.name: Mustafa Badreddine
eliminated.title: Hezbollah Military Commander (succeeded Imad Mughniyeh)
eliminated.method: Explosion at Damascus airport — circumstances disputed (Israel/internal Hezbollah attributed)
eliminated.operationName: Disputed — attributed to Israeli strike or internal dispute
eliminated.significance: Hezbollah's top military commander and successor to Mughniyeh. Convicted in absentia by the Special Tribunal for Lebanon for the 2005 assassination of Lebanese PM Rafik Hariri. Oversaw Hezbollah's military intervention in the Syrian civil war supporting Assad.

eliminated.name: Samir Kuntar
eliminated.title: Senior Hezbollah Operative; Golan Heights Operations Commander
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force precision strike
eliminated.significance: Longest-held Lebanese prisoner in Israel (1979-2008), convicted for the 1979 Nahariya attack that killed 4 Israelis including a 4-year-old. After release in a prisoner swap, joined Hezbollah and was appointed to coordinate attacks from the Golan Heights against Israel.

eliminated.name: Ali Karaki
eliminated.title: Commander of Hezbollah's Southern Front
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force bunker-buster strike (same strike that killed Nasrallah)
eliminated.significance: Commander of Hezbollah's southern front, responsible for all military operations along the Israel-Lebanon border. Killed in the same massive Israeli airstrike that eliminated Nasrallah. Oversaw the cross-border attacks on Israel that began on October 8, 2023.

eliminated.name: Wissam al-Tawil
eliminated.title: Deputy Commander of Hezbollah's Radwan Force
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli drone strike
eliminated.significance: Senior deputy commander of Hezbollah's elite Radwan Force. One of the highest-ranking Hezbollah military figures killed in the early phase of the Israel-Hezbollah cross-border conflict. The Radwan Force was Hezbollah's most capable offensive unit, trained for ground invasion of northern Israel.

eliminated.name: Muhammad Nasser
eliminated.title: Commander of Hezbollah's Aziz Unit (southwestern Lebanon front)
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force precision strike
eliminated.significance: Commanded Hezbollah's Aziz Unit responsible for operations in southwestern Lebanon along the Israeli border. The most senior Hezbollah commander killed in southern Lebanon before the September 2024 escalation.

eliminated.name: Taleb Abdallah (Abu Taleb)
eliminated.title: Senior Hezbollah Military Commander — Southern Lebanon
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force precision strike
eliminated.significance: Most senior Hezbollah commander killed in cross-border fighting at the time. His death triggered Hezbollah's heaviest rocket barrage into northern Israel. Commanded a sector of Hezbollah's southern front and oversaw daily attacks on Israeli border positions.

eliminated.name: Ahmed Wahbi
eliminated.title: Radwan Force Commander — killed alongside Ibrahim Aqil
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force precision strike on Beirut
eliminated.operationName: Same strike that killed Ibrahim Aqil
eliminated.significance: Senior Radwan Force commander killed in the same Israeli strike that targeted Ibrahim Aqil during a meeting of Hezbollah military leadership. Multiple Radwan commanders were eliminated in this single strike, devastating Hezbollah's elite special operations capability.

eliminated.name: Nabil Kaouk
eliminated.title: Deputy Head of Hezbollah's Central Council
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force strike
eliminated.operationName: Israeli targeted strike (day after Nasrallah's assassination)
eliminated.significance: Deputy head of Hezbollah's Central Council and one of the group's most prominent military-political figures. Long-time commander who oversaw Hezbollah operations in southern Lebanon for decades. His killing the day after Nasrallah demonstrated the depth of Israel's intelligence penetration.

eliminated.name: Muhammad Surur
eliminated.title: Head of Hezbollah's Aerial Operations (drone/missile program)
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force precision strike
eliminated.significance: Led Hezbollah's drone and precision missile programs. Oversaw the development and deployment of Hezbollah's expanding UAV fleet and precision-guided munitions capability that posed a growing threat to Israeli critical infrastructure.

eliminated.name: Muhammad Haydar
eliminated.title: Radwan Force Senior Commander
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force precision strike
eliminated.significance: Senior commander in Hezbollah's elite Radwan Force, killed in the intensive Israeli campaign against Hezbollah's military leadership in September 2024. The Radwan Force suffered devastating leadership losses during this period.

eliminated.name: Abbas Nilforoushan
eliminated.title: Deputy Commander of IRGC Operations; Senior IRGC Quds Force Commander
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force bunker-buster strike (killed alongside Nasrallah)
eliminated.operationName: Same strike that killed Hassan Nasrallah
eliminated.significance: Senior IRGC commander killed alongside Nasrallah in the Israeli strike on Hezbollah's underground headquarters. His presence with Nasrallah at the time of the strike confirmed the deep integration of IRGC leadership within Hezbollah's command structure. His death was the highest-ranking IRGC casualty since Soleimani.

eliminated.name: Hassan Mahdavi
eliminated.title: IRGC Quds Force Commander — Lebanon/Syria Theater
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force strike in Beirut
eliminated.operationName: Israeli targeted strike during Lebanon campaign
eliminated.significance: IRGC Quds Force commander responsible for coordinating Iranian support to Hezbollah in Lebanon and Syria. His killing further disrupted the IRGC's command link to its most important proxy organization.

eliminated.name: Abu Muhammad al-Adnani
eliminated.title: Official Spokesman of ISIS; Head of External Operations
eliminated.method: Airstrike — US precision strike (also claimed by Russia)
eliminated.significance: ISIS's chief spokesman and head of its external operations division. Declared the ISIS caliphate in June 2014. Directed and inspired the wave of ISIS terror attacks in Europe, including the November 2015 Paris attacks (130 killed) and the March 2016 Brussels bombings (32 killed). One of the most dangerous ISIS leaders after Baghdadi.

eliminated.name: Mohammed Emwazi (Jihadi John)
eliminated.title: ISIS Executioner; Member of 'The Beatles' cell
eliminated.method: US drone strike — MQ-9 Reaper precision strike on vehicle
eliminated.significance: British-Kuwaiti ISIS member who appeared in multiple propaganda videos beheading Western hostages, including American journalists James Foley and Steven Sotloff, and British aid workers David Haines and Alan Henning. His masked image became the face of ISIS brutality worldwide.

eliminated.name: Abu Omar al-Shishani
eliminated.title: ISIS Minister of War / Senior Military Commander
eliminated.method: US airstrike — followed by death from wounds
eliminated.significance: Georgian-born Chechen jihadist who served as ISIS's top military commander. Known as 'Omar the Chechen,' he led ISIS forces in key battles across Syria and Iraq. One of the most recognizable ISIS figures due to his distinctive red beard. Commanded thousands of foreign fighters.

eliminated.name: Abu Sayyaf
eliminated.title: Head of ISIS Oil and Gas Operations / Senior Financial Official
eliminated.method: US Special Operations raid — Delta Force ground assault on compound
eliminated.operationName: Delta Force raid — authorized by President Obama
eliminated.significance: Oversaw ISIS's oil and gas revenue operations, which generated hundreds of millions of dollars annually. Killed during a rare US ground raid in eastern Syria. His wife, Umm Sayyaf, was captured and provided significant intelligence on ISIS operations and the captivity of Kayla Mueller.

eliminated.name: Turki al-Binali
eliminated.title: Grand Mufti (Chief Religious Authority) of ISIS
eliminated.method: US-led coalition airstrike
eliminated.significance: Bahraini cleric who served as ISIS's most senior religious authority. Provided the theological justifications for ISIS's most extreme practices including slavery, mass execution, and the destruction of historical sites. His religious rulings gave ISIS's atrocities a veneer of Islamic legitimacy.

eliminated.name: Abu al-Hassan al-Hashimi al-Qurayshi
eliminated.title: Leader of the Islamic State (3rd Caliph)
eliminated.method: Killed in combat — Free Syrian Army operation in Daraa, Syria
eliminated.significance: Third leader of ISIS following the deaths of Baghdadi and Abu Ibrahim. Led ISIS for approximately 8 months. His death continued the pattern of rapid leadership turnover that degraded ISIS's ability to maintain centralized command.

eliminated.name: Maher al-Agal
eliminated.title: Leader of ISIS in Syria; Senior ISIS Overall Leader
eliminated.method: US drone strike — MQ-9 Reaper Hellfire R9X missile
eliminated.operationName: US targeted strike — authorized by President Biden
eliminated.significance: Led ISIS operations across all of Syria and was considered one of the top five overall ISIS leaders globally. Responsible for developing ISIS's network in Turkey and aggressively pursuing plans to reconstitute ISIS's capability. Killed by the 'flying Ginsu' R9X blade-equipped Hellfire missile.

eliminated.name: Abu Ala al-Afri
eliminated.title: Deputy Leader / Acting Leader of ISIS
eliminated.method: US Special Operations raid on compound
eliminated.significance: Former physics teacher who became ISIS's deputy leader and designated successor to Baghdadi. Oversaw ISIS's administrative and bureaucratic operations. Would have assumed leadership if Baghdadi had been killed.

eliminated.name: Abu Muslim al-Turkmani
eliminated.title: Deputy Leader of ISIS for Iraq; Head of Military Council
eliminated.method: US-led coalition airstrike
eliminated.significance: Former Iraqi army officer under Saddam Hussein who became one of ISIS's two deputy leaders. Oversaw all ISIS military operations in Iraq. His military experience from the former Iraqi army was crucial to ISIS's early battlefield success.

eliminated.name: Haji Bakr
eliminated.title: Head of ISIS Military Council; Chief Strategist
eliminated.method: Killed by Syrian rebels during raid on his safehouse
eliminated.significance: Former colonel in Saddam Hussein's military intelligence who became the strategic mastermind behind ISIS's rise. Designed the blueprint for ISIS's takeover of Syrian territory using intelligence cells, informant networks, and systematic infiltration. Documents found after his death revealed ISIS's detailed plan for state-building through terror.

eliminated.name: Abu Wahib
eliminated.title: ISIS Military Commander — Anbar Province
eliminated.method: US airstrike on vehicle convoy
eliminated.significance: Notorious ISIS military commander in Anbar Province. Known for his brutality, he appeared in multiple propaganda videos executing prisoners. Commanded ISIS forces in key battles for control of western Iraq's Sunni heartland.

eliminated.name: Anwar al-Awlaki
eliminated.title: Senior Leader of Al-Qaeda in the Arabian Peninsula (AQAP); Chief External Operations Planner
eliminated.method: CIA drone strike — MQ-9 Reaper / MQ-1 Predator firing Hellfire missiles
eliminated.operationName: CIA targeted strike — authorized by President Obama
eliminated.significance: American-born Yemeni cleric who became the most dangerous English-language recruiter for Al-Qaeda. Directed the 2009 Christmas Day underwear bomber plot, the 2010 cargo plane bomb plot, and inspired the Fort Hood shooting. The first US citizen deliberately targeted and killed by the US government without trial, raising significant legal debate.

eliminated.name: Abu Yahya al-Libi
eliminated.title: Deputy Leader of Al-Qaeda; Chief Religious Authority
eliminated.method: CIA drone strike — Predator/Reaper strike
eliminated.significance: Al-Qaeda's #2 leader and its most prominent religious authority. Escaped from Bagram Air Base in Afghanistan in 2005. His theological writings provided Al-Qaeda's ideological foundation. His death was described as the most significant strike against Al-Qaeda since bin Laden's killing.

eliminated.name: Nasir al-Wuhayshi
eliminated.title: Leader of Al-Qaeda in the Arabian Peninsula (AQAP); Al-Qaeda General Manager
eliminated.method: US drone strike — CIA/JSOC strike on vehicle
eliminated.significance: Former personal secretary to Osama bin Laden who went on to lead AQAP, considered Al-Qaeda's most dangerous franchise. Also served as Al-Qaeda's global 'general manager' — effectively the #2 leader of the entire Al-Qaeda network. Under his leadership, AQAP directed multiple attacks on the US homeland and orchestrated the Charlie Hebdo attack in Paris.

eliminated.name: Qasim al-Raymi
eliminated.title: Leader of Al-Qaeda in the Arabian Peninsula (AQAP)
eliminated.method: US drone strike — CIA airstrike
eliminated.operationName: CIA targeted strike — confirmed by President Trump
eliminated.significance: Succeeded al-Wuhayshi as AQAP leader. Claimed responsibility for the December 2019 Pensacola Naval Air Station shooting. Under his leadership, AQAP remained the Al-Qaeda franchise most focused on attacking the US homeland.

eliminated.name: Atiyah Abd al-Rahman
eliminated.title: Al-Qaeda Deputy Leader / Operations Chief
eliminated.method: CIA drone strike
eliminated.significance: Libyan national who served as Al-Qaeda's operations chief and became the de facto #2 after bin Laden's death. Managed day-to-day operations of Al-Qaeda's network. Killed less than four months after bin Laden, dealing back-to-back blows to Al-Qaeda's core leadership.

eliminated.name: Abu Khayr al-Masri
eliminated.title: Deputy Leader of Al-Qaeda (under Zawahiri)
eliminated.method: US drone strike — precision strike on vehicle
eliminated.significance: Egyptian national who served as Al-Qaeda's deputy overall leader. Son-in-law of Zawahiri. Previously held in Iran alongside other senior Al-Qaeda figures before relocating to Syria. His killing in an area controlled by Hayat Tahrir al-Sham exposed ongoing Al-Qaeda presence in Syria.

eliminated.name: Jamal al-Badawi
eliminated.title: Al-Qaeda Operative; Mastermind of USS Cole Bombing
eliminated.method: US airstrike — precision strike
eliminated.operationName: US targeted strike — confirmed by President Trump
eliminated.significance: Mastermind of the October 2000 bombing of the USS Cole in Aden, Yemen, which killed 17 US sailors. On the FBI's Most Wanted Terrorists list. Escaped from Yemeni prison twice. His killing brought closure to the families of the Cole bombing victims after nearly two decades.

eliminated.name: Fazul Abdullah Mohammed
eliminated.title: Al-Qaeda East Africa Operations Chief
eliminated.method: Killed at Somali government checkpoint — shot during vehicle stop
eliminated.significance: Al-Qaeda's senior operative in East Africa. Mastermind of the 1998 US Embassy bombings in Nairobi, Kenya (213 killed) and Dar es Salaam, Tanzania (11 killed). Also planned the 2002 Mombasa attacks. On the FBI Most Wanted list for over a decade. Accidentally drove into a government checkpoint and was killed in the ensuing shootout.

eliminated.name: Said al-Shihri
eliminated.title: Deputy Leader of AQAP
eliminated.method: US drone strike
eliminated.significance: Saudi national and former Guantanamo Bay detainee who became AQAP's deputy leader after release through Saudi rehabilitation program. His case became the most prominent example of terrorist recidivism and a major argument against Guantanamo releases.

eliminated.name: Abu Laith al-Libi
eliminated.title: Senior Al-Qaeda Military Commander
eliminated.method: CIA drone strike — Predator strike on compound
eliminated.significance: Senior Al-Qaeda military commander who appeared regularly in propaganda videos. Linked to the 2007 suicide bombing at Bagram Air Base during Vice President Cheney's visit. One of the earliest high-value targets eliminated in the CIA's expanded drone campaign in Pakistan.

eliminated.name: Saeed al-Masri
eliminated.title: Al-Qaeda #3 / Chief Financial Officer
eliminated.method: CIA drone strike
eliminated.significance: Egyptian national who served as Al-Qaeda's third-ranking leader and chief financial officer. Managed Al-Qaeda's financial networks and fundraising operations. A founding member of Egyptian Islamic Jihad before merging with Al-Qaeda.

eliminated.name: Ahmed Abdi Godane
eliminated.title: Emir (Leader) of Al-Shabaab
eliminated.method: US airstrike — multiple Hellfire missiles and precision bombs
eliminated.significance: Founder and first leader of Al-Shabaab. Pledged allegiance to Al-Qaeda in 2012. Under his leadership, Al-Shabaab controlled most of south-central Somalia, conducted the 2013 Westgate mall attack in Nairobi (67 killed), and implemented brutal governance. His death led to Ahmed Diriye's succession.

eliminated.name: Adan Garar
eliminated.title: Al-Shabaab Intelligence Chief; External Operations Planner
eliminated.method: US drone strike
eliminated.significance: Al-Shabaab's intelligence chief who planned the 2013 Westgate shopping mall attack in Nairobi that killed 67 people. Also planned attacks against Western targets across East Africa. His elimination disrupted Al-Shabaab's external operations capability.

eliminated.name: Abdishakur (Tahlil)
eliminated.title: Al-Shabaab Senior Leader; External Operations Planner
eliminated.method: US drone strike
eliminated.significance: Senior Al-Shabaab leader responsible for planning attacks outside Somalia, particularly targeting Kenya. Coordinated Al-Shabaab's external operations network that facilitated cross-border attacks.

eliminated.name: Abdirahman Sandhere (Ukash)
eliminated.title: Al-Shabaab Intelligence and Security Chief
eliminated.method: US drone strike
eliminated.significance: Head of Al-Shabaab's Amniyat (intelligence and security wing), the organization's most secretive and feared division. The Amniyat was responsible for assassinations, intelligence collection, and Al-Shabaab's most sensitive operations.

eliminated.name: Bilal al-Sudani
eliminated.title: ISIS East Africa External Operations Chief (operating within Al-Shabaab territory)
eliminated.method: US Special Operations raid — SEAL Team Six ground assault
eliminated.operationName: US JSOC raid — authorized by President Biden
eliminated.significance: Senior ISIS leader who operated within Al-Shabaab's territory managing ISIS's global financial network and external attack planning from East Africa. Killed during a rare US ground raid — the first significant US ground operation in Somalia in years. His death disrupted ISIS's financial facilitation network.

eliminated.name: Mullah Akhtar Mansour
eliminated.title: Supreme Leader of the Taliban (succeeded Mullah Omar)
eliminated.method: US drone strike — MQ-9 Reaper strike on vehicle crossing Pakistan border
eliminated.location: Ahmad Wal, Balochistan, Pakistan (near Afghanistan border)
eliminated.operationName: US targeted strike — authorized by President Obama
eliminated.significance: Led the Taliban following the revelation of Mullah Omar's death. Under his leadership, the Taliban expanded territorial control across Afghanistan. His killing inside Pakistan was significant — the first US drone strike on a Taliban leader in Balochistan rather than the tribal areas, demonstrating US willingness to strike deeper into Pakistan.

eliminated.name: Mullah Dadullah
eliminated.title: Taliban Supreme Military Commander
eliminated.method: Killed in joint US-Afghan special operations raid
eliminated.significance: The Taliban's most aggressive and feared military commander. Known for extreme brutality including beheading prisoners on video (inspired by Al-Qaeda in Iraq tactics). Commanded Taliban forces in southern Afghanistan. One-legged from a prior injury but remained a fearsome field commander.

eliminated.name: Baitullah Mehsud
eliminated.title: Founder and First Leader of Tehrik-i-Taliban Pakistan (TTP)
eliminated.method: CIA drone strike — Predator strike on rooftop while receiving IV drip
eliminated.significance: Founded the TTP in 2007, unifying Pakistani tribal militant factions. Responsible for the assassination of former Pakistani PM Benazir Bhutto in December 2007. Under his leadership, TTP conducted devastating suicide bombings across Pakistan. Killed by a drone strike while on the roof of his father-in-law's house.

eliminated.name: Hakimullah Mehsud
eliminated.title: Leader of Tehrik-i-Taliban Pakistan (TTP); succeeded Baitullah Mehsud
eliminated.method: CIA drone strike — Predator/Reaper strike on compound
eliminated.significance: Led the TTP from 2009-2013. Mastermind of the 2010 Times Square car bomb attempt. Under his leadership, TTP intensified attacks across Pakistan. Directed the 2009 Camp Chapman suicide bombing at a CIA base in Afghanistan that killed 7 CIA officers — the deadliest attack on the CIA in decades.

eliminated.name: Abu Mahdi al-Muhandis
eliminated.title: Deputy Commander of Iraq's Popular Mobilization Forces; Leader of Kata'ib Hezbollah
eliminated.method: US drone strike — MQ-9 Reaper Hellfire missiles (same strike that killed Soleimani)
eliminated.operationName: Same operation that killed Qasem Soleimani
eliminated.significance: Leader of Kata'ib Hezbollah and deputy commander of Iraq's PMF. Killed in the same US drone strike that assassinated Qasem Soleimani. A US-designated terrorist since 2009. Directed multiple rocket and IED attacks on US forces in Iraq. His dual role as both a designated terrorist and a senior Iraqi government-linked militia leader exemplified Iran's infiltration of Iraqi state structures.

eliminated.name: Abu Baqir al-Saadi
eliminated.title: Senior Kata'ib Hezbollah Commander
eliminated.method: US drone strike — MQ-9 Reaper precision strike on vehicle
eliminated.operationName: US retaliatory strike following Tower 22 attack
eliminated.significance: Senior Kata'ib Hezbollah commander killed in a US retaliatory strike following the January 28, 2024 drone attack on Tower 22 in Jordan that killed 3 US soldiers. The US strike in central Baghdad demonstrated willingness to hit Iranian-backed militia leaders in Iraq's capital.

eliminated.name: Dmitry Utkin
eliminated.title: Co-Founder and Military Commander of Wagner Group
eliminated.method: Aircraft crash — killed on same plane as Prigozhin
eliminated.operationName: Same incident as Prigozhin — attributed to Russian state
eliminated.significance: Former Russian GRU special forces lieutenant colonel who co-founded and served as the military commander of Wagner Group. His call sign 'Wagner' gave the group its name. Led Wagner's early operations in eastern Ukraine (2014) and Syria. Killed alongside Prigozhin when their plane was shot down, eliminating both founders of the organization.

eliminated.name: Abu Musab al-Barnawi
eliminated.title: Leader of ISWAP (Islamic State West Africa Province)
eliminated.method: Reported killed in internal clash / Nigerian military operation (circumstances disputed)
eliminated.significance: Son of Boko Haram founder Mohammed Yusuf. Led the ISWAP faction that split from Boko Haram in 2016 and pledged allegiance to ISIS. Under his leadership, ISWAP became the more organized and strategically effective faction, focusing on military targets rather than civilian massacres. His death disrupted ISWAP's chain of command.

eliminated.name: Ahmed Jabari
eliminated.title: Chief of Staff of the Izz ad-Din al-Qassam Brigades
eliminated.method: Israeli airstrike — precision strike on vehicle
eliminated.operationName: Opening strike of Operation Pillar of Defense
eliminated.significance: Head of Hamas' military wing. The Israeli strike on his car in central Gaza City initiated Operation Pillar of Defense (2012). Jabari had been Hamas' top military leader for years, overseeing rocket attacks on Israel and the capture of Israeli soldier Gilad Shalit in 2006. His assassination was one of Israel's most prominent targeted killings.

eliminated.name: Sheikh Ahmed Yassin
eliminated.title: Founder and Spiritual Leader of Hamas
eliminated.method: Israeli helicopter gunship strike — Hellfire missiles while leaving mosque
eliminated.operationName: Israeli targeted strike — authorized by PM Ariel Sharon
eliminated.significance: Founder of Hamas in 1987 and its supreme spiritual authority. Quadriplegic cleric who led Hamas from its founding through the Second Intifada's suicide bombing campaign. His assassination was one of the most controversial Israeli targeted killings, sparking massive protests across the Muslim world. His death was followed weeks later by the assassination of his successor, Abdel Aziz al-Rantisi.

eliminated.name: Abdel Aziz al-Rantisi
eliminated.title: Leader of Hamas (succeeded Sheikh Yassin)
eliminated.method: Israeli helicopter gunship strike — Hellfire missiles on vehicle
eliminated.significance: Co-founder of Hamas and successor to Sheikh Yassin as Hamas leader. A pediatrician who became one of Hamas' most hardline leaders. Assassinated just 26 days after succeeding Yassin, demonstrating Israel's determination to decapitate Hamas' leadership. His rapid elimination led Hamas to stop publicly announcing successors.

eliminated.name: Mahmoud al-Mabhouh
eliminated.title: Co-Founder of the Izz ad-Din al-Qassam Brigades; Senior Hamas Military Commander
eliminated.method: Assassination — Mossad team administered muscle relaxant and suffocated him in hotel room
eliminated.location: Al Bustan Rotana Hotel, Dubai, UAE
eliminated.operationName: Mossad operation (confirmed by Dubai police investigation)
eliminated.significance: Co-founder of Hamas' military wing and a senior weapons procurement officer responsible for smuggling Iranian arms into Gaza. His assassination in Dubai by a Mossad team using forged passports became one of the most documented intelligence operations in history — Dubai police released CCTV footage tracking the entire Mossad team.

eliminated.name: Ibrahim al-Makadmeh
eliminated.title: Founder of the Izz ad-Din al-Qassam Brigades
eliminated.method: Israeli helicopter gunship strike — Hellfire missile on vehicle
eliminated.significance: One of the founders of Hamas' military wing, the Izz ad-Din al-Qassam Brigades. A key figure in building Hamas' armed capability during the First and Second Intifadas. His assassination was part of Israel's systematic targeting of Hamas' military leadership.

eliminated.name: Salah Shehade
eliminated.title: Commander of the Izz ad-Din al-Qassam Brigades
eliminated.method: Israeli Air Force — F-16 dropped 1-ton bomb on residential building
eliminated.significance: Founder and commander of the Qassam Brigades. Under his command, the military wing carried out numerous suicide bombings and attacks during the Second Intifada. The strike that killed him also killed 14 civilians, making it one of the most controversial Israeli targeted killings.

eliminated.name: Yahya Ayyash (The Engineer)
eliminated.title: Chief Bombmaker of Hamas; Master of Suicide Bombing Technology
eliminated.method: Assassination — Shin Bet planted explosive in mobile phone
eliminated.significance: Known as 'The Engineer' — Hamas' master bomb-maker who revolutionized suicide bombing in the Israeli-Palestinian conflict. Built the bombs used in a wave of bus bombings that killed dozens of Israelis in 1994-1996. Assassinated when a booby-trapped mobile phone exploded against his head. His death triggered retaliatory Hamas bombings that killed 60 Israelis in February-March 1996.

eliminated.name: Abu Musab al-Zarqawi
eliminated.title: Leader of Al-Qaeda in Iraq (AQI) — predecessor organization to ISIS
eliminated.method: US airstrike — two 500-lb GBU-12 laser-guided bombs dropped by F-16
eliminated.operationName: US targeted strike based on intelligence from Jordanian agent
eliminated.significance: Jordanian jihadist who founded Al-Qaeda in Iraq (AQI), the precursor to ISIS. Known as 'The Sheikh of the Slaughterers' for his campaign of beheadings, car bombings, and sectarian massacres that killed thousands. Mastermind of the 2005 Amman hotel bombings. His organization would later evolve into ISIS under Baghdadi.

eliminated.name: Muhsin al-Fadhli
eliminated.title: Leader of the Khorasan Group (Al-Qaeda external operations cell in Syria)
eliminated.method: US airstrike — precision strike
eliminated.significance: Kuwaiti national who led the 'Khorasan Group,' an Al-Qaeda external operations cell embedded within Jabhat al-Nusra in Syria. One of the few people who had advance knowledge of the 9/11 attacks. Was planning imminent attacks against Western aviation when killed.

eliminated.name: Amadou Koufa
eliminated.title: Leader of Katiba Macina (JNIM's central Mali branch)
eliminated.method: French/Malian military operation (circumstances disputed — reported killed multiple times)
eliminated.significance: Fulani cleric who founded Katiba Macina, the most active insurgent group in central Mali. Exploited ethnic Fulani grievances to recruit fighters. His group was responsible for the massive expansion of the Sahel insurgency into central Mali and neighboring Burkina Faso. One of JNIM's most operationally active commanders.

eliminated.name: Fathi Hammad
eliminated.title: Senior Hamas Political Bureau Member; Former Interior Minister
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force strike
eliminated.significance: Senior Hamas political leader and former Interior Minister of the Hamas government in Gaza. Known for inflammatory speeches calling for attacks on Israelis. One of the few Hamas political bureau members who remained in Gaza during the war.

eliminated.name: Nizar Awadallah
eliminated.title: Head of Hamas' Economic Portfolio; Senior Political Figure
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force precision strike
eliminated.significance: Managed Hamas' economic affairs and investment portfolios. Oversaw the financial infrastructure that sustained Hamas operations during the war, including fund disbursement to fighters' families and operational expenses.

eliminated.name: Izz al-Din al-Haddad
eliminated.title: Commander of Hamas' Drone Operations Unit
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force strike on concealed drone facility
eliminated.significance: Led Hamas' expanding drone warfare program, which deployed reconnaissance and attack drones during the October 7 assault and throughout the subsequent war. Hamas used drones to drop munitions on IDF positions and for reconnaissance of Israeli troop movements.

eliminated.name: Bassem Issa
eliminated.title: Commander of Hamas' Gaza City Brigade (Qassam Brigades)
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force precision strike during Operation Guardian of the Walls
eliminated.operationName: IDF targeted strike — Operation Guardian of the Walls (May 2021)
eliminated.significance: Highest-ranking Hamas military commander killed during the May 2021 Gaza conflict. Commanded all Qassam Brigades operations in Gaza City. His elimination during the 11-day war demonstrated Israel's ability to identify and strike Hamas' brigade-level commanders even during active combat.

active.name: Ali Khamenei
active.title: Supreme Leader of Iran; Commander-in-Chief of all Iranian armed forces including IRGC
active.description: Supreme Leader of Iran since 1989. Ultimate authority over the IRGC and Iran's proxy network. Authorized the April 2024 direct missile and drone attack on Israel — the first direct Iranian strike on Israeli territory. Controls Iran's nuclear program and 'Axis of Resistance' strategy.

active.name: Esmail Qaani
active.title: Commander of the IRGC Quds Force (succeeded Soleimani)
active.description: Succeeded Qasem Soleimani as commander of the IRGC Quds Force. Manages Iran's proxy relationships across the Middle East. Less charismatic and operationally aggressive than Soleimani but maintains the proxy network. Reportedly suffered a cardiac event in late 2024 and his current operational status is unclear.
knownAssociates: Iranian proxy militia commanders across Iraq, Syria, Lebanon, Yemen

active.name: Abdul Malik al-Houthi
active.title: Leader of the Houthi movement / Ansar Allah
active.lastKnownLocation: Undisclosed location in northern Yemen (Saada region)
active.description: Political and religious leader of the Houthi movement since 2004 (following his brother Hussein's death). Commands the Houthi forces conducting the Red Sea anti-shipping campaign. Portrays himself as defender of Palestinian cause. Has escalated attacks despite US/UK military strikes on Houthi positions.

active.name: Naim Qassem
active.title: Secretary-General of Hezbollah (succeeded Nasrallah, elected October 2024)
active.description: Long-time deputy to Hassan Nasrallah. Elected as Hezbollah's new Secretary-General following the assassinations of Nasrallah and Safieddine. Theologian and political figure rather than military commander. Faces the challenge of rebuilding Hezbollah's command structure after Israel's decapitation campaign.

active.name: Abu Hafs al-Hashimi al-Qurayshi
active.title: Leader of the Islamic State (current)
active.description: Current leader of ISIS, announced in August 2023. Little is publicly known about his identity or background. Leads an organization that continues to conduct insurgent attacks in Syria and Iraq while maintaining global affiliate networks across Africa and Central Asia.

active.name: Ahmed Diriye
active.title: Emir (Leader) of Al-Shabaab
active.description: Leader of Al-Shabaab since 2014, formally pledged allegiance to Al-Qaeda. Has expanded Al-Shabaab's revenue base and territorial control despite sustained US and AMISOM military pressure. Believed to be in hiding in southern Somalia with limited public appearances.

active.name: Iyad ag Ghaly
active.title: Leader of JNIM (Jama'at Nusrat al-Islam wal-Muslimin)
active.description: Tuareg leader who founded Ansar Dine and now leads JNIM, Al-Qaeda's Sahel affiliate. Former Malian diplomat turned jihadist. Has unified multiple jihadist factions across the Sahel into the most potent insurgent force in the region. The most wanted person in West Africa.

active.name: Saif al-Adel
active.title: De facto leader of Al-Qaeda (unconfirmed formal appointment)
active.lastKnownLocation: Iran (under IRGC monitoring / loose house arrest since 2003)
active.description: Egyptian former special forces officer. Al-Qaeda's military chief and likely successor to al-Zawahiri. Has been in Iran since 2003 under a complex arrangement with the IRGC. Wanted for his role in the 1998 US Embassy bombings in Kenya and Tanzania.

active.name: Hibatullah Akhundzada
active.title: Supreme Leader (Amir al-Mu'minin) of the Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan
active.description: Supreme leader of the Taliban since 2016 and de facto ruler of Afghanistan since the August 2021 takeover. Reclusive Islamic scholar who has made very few public appearances. Has imposed the world's most restrictive policies on women and girls, banning secondary education and most employment.

active.name: Sirajuddin Haqqani
active.title: Minister of Interior, Taliban government; Leader of the Haqqani Network
active.description: Leader of the Haqqani Network, designated by the US as a Foreign Terrorist Organization. Now serves as Afghanistan's Interior Minister. Responsible for some of the deadliest attacks in Afghanistan. He is a US-designated terrorist who now runs a government ministry.

active.name: Noor Wali Mehsud
active.title: Emir (Leader) of Tehrik-i-Taliban Pakistan (TTP)
active.description: Leader of the TTP since June 2018. Has reunified TTP splinter factions and intensified attacks against Pakistan, especially since the Taliban takeover of Afghanistan which provided TTP with sanctuary.
